text:
BREAST ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Marlena Okonkwo
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Established patient follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine follow-up following completion of adjuvant cape (6 cycles). pembro maintenance ongoing.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 38-39 year-old woman with a history of stage IIB (cT2 N1 M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (upper outer quadrant, grade 3, Ki-67 85%, PD-L1 CPS 15 by 22C3, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant p.E1250*), who presents today for oncology follow-up now that she has completed all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant cape (CREATE-X regimen).

To briefly summarize her oncologic course: She was diagnosed with TNBC approximately 10-11 months ago after self-detecting a left breast mass. She received neoadjuvant therapy per a KEYNOTE-522-inspired regimen: dose-dense AC x 4 cycles concurrently with Keytruda 200 mg q3 weeks, followed by weekly pacli x 12 weeks with concurrent pembro. Neoadjuvant course was complicated by grade 2 immune-mediated hepatitis (pembro held, prednisone administered, LFTs normalized, pembro successfully resumed), grade 2 CINV during ddAC, and grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy during the taxane phase (Taxol dose reduced 20% for the final 4 doses; duloxetine initiated). She went on to left lumpectomy with SLNB/ALND; final pathology showed ypT1c ypN0(i+), residual 1.4 cm IDC with 55% treatment effect, RCB class II, Miller-Payne grade 3. She then completed adjuvant WBRT with nodal irradiation (50 Gy + 10 Gy boost) with concurrent grade 2 radiation dermatitis, managed topically without treatment interruption. Following radiation she was started on adjuvant cape per CREATE-X (1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 q21 days) plus adjuvant pembro maintenance (continuing from neoadjuvant; this is now cycle 14 total).

cape course: She completed all 6 cycles. Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (HFS) -- bilateral palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia with pain, erythema, and swelling interfering with prolonged standing (patient is a high school biology teacher). Cape was dose-reduced 25% to 750 mg/m2 BID per protocol. Topical urea 20% cream and pyridoxine 200 mg daily were added. HFS improved to grade 1 within 2 weeks. Cycles 3-6 were administered at the reduced dose (750 mg/m2 BID) without further dose modifications. She has now completed her final (cycle 6, day 14) dose approximately 2 weeks ago.

Current symptoms: She reports persistent mild fatigue, which she rates as improved compared to the middle of radiation. She denies any active nausea. Neuropathy is stable -- describes persistent numbness and tingling in her feet (bilateral), denies progression, continues on duloxetine 60 mg daily. She denies any new neurologic symptoms. No fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, or abdominal pain. She reports mild constipation over the past few weeks, which she attributes to dietary changes. She notes some weight gain of approximately 3-4 lbs over the past 2 months and mild cold intolerance. She denies palpitations or tremor.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (grade 1, improved from prior). Mild cold intolerance. Weight gain ~3-4 lbs over 2 months. No fever, night sweats.
- HEENT: No headache, no visual changes.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, no cough.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
- GI: Mild constipation. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. No rectal bleeding. Resolving palmar-plantar tenderness (residual from HFS, continuing to improve).
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No joint swelling.
- Neurologic: Stable grade 1 bilateral foot neuropathy. No new deficits.
- Dermatologic: Residual grade 1 HFS bilaterally, ongoing improvement. No new rash or skin changes.
- Endocrine: Cold intolerance, constipation, mild fatigue -- see below.
- Psychiatric: Mood intact; she reports some anxiety around surveillance but is coping well and has returned to full-time teaching this semester.

---

MEDICATIONS:
- Keytruda (Keytruda) 200 mg IV q3 weeks (cycle 14, continuing adjuvant maintenance)
- Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 60 mg PO daily (for chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy)
- Albuterol HFA 90 mcg MDI 2 puffs q4-6h PRN (mild intermittent asthma, rarely used)
- Urea 20% cream topically BID to palms and soles (HFS prophylaxis/treatment, tapering use)
- Pyridoxine 200 mg PO daily (HFS adjunct)
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN (nausea, now using rarely)
- Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO q6h PRN (breakthrough antiemetic; rarely using)
- Vitamin D 1000 IU PO daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, stage IIB (cT2 N1 M0), as above
- Germline BRCA1 p.E1250* pathogenic variant (reported to genetic counseling; sisters referred for testing)
- Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, grade 1 (currently stable on duloxetine)
- Immune-mediated hepatitis grade 2 (resolved, attributed to pembro, during neoadjuvant phase)
- Mild intermittent asthma

SOCIAL HISTORY:
High school biology teacher; returned to full-time work this semester. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol (currently not drinking). Nulliparous. West African descent. Resides with partner. Sisters have been referred for BRCA1 germline testing.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Maternal aunt: ovarian cancer, age 52. Maternal grandmother: breast cancer, age 60.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Well-appearing woman, no acute distress. Appears mildly fatigued. Hair regrowth visible (approximately 2-3 cm, post-chemo alopecia). Alert and oriented.
Vital signs: BP 112/70, HR 62 bpm, RR 14, Temp 36.7 C, O2 sat 98% on room air. Weight: [documented]. BMI: [documented].
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx clear. No mucositis. Thyroid: mildly enlarged, non-tender, diffusely smooth, no nodularity appreciated on palpation. No thyroid bruit.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or right axillary adenopathy. Left axilla: post-ALND changes, no palpable adenopathy.
Breasts: Right breast without palpable mass, skin changes, or nipple discharge. Left breast: post-lumpectomy scar in the upper outer quadrant, well-healed. No palpable residual mass at the lumpectomy site or elsewhere in the left breast. Expected post-radiation skin changes -- mild hyperpigmentation in the left breast, resolving.
Cardiopulmonary: Regular rate and rhythm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness. Mild residual skin changes at bilateral palms and soles consistent with resolving grade 1 HFS.
Neurologic: Sensation mildly reduced in a stocking distribution bilaterally at the feet (consistent with grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, stable per patient report). Normal upper extremity sensation and grip strength. No ataxia.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):

CBC:
- WBC 6.1 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL (low-normal, baseline was ~12.8 pre-treatment; mild anemia, grade 1 if confirmed trend)
- Hematocrit 35.4%
- Platelets 218 K/uL (normal)
- MCV 91 fL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 101, CO2 24 (normal)
- BUN 14, Creatinine 0.82 (normal)
- AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L (normal -- LFTs remain normal since recovery from immune hepatitis)
- Alk phos 72 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin 3.9 g/dL (normal)
- Glucose 91 mg/dL (normal)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH 6.8 mIU/L (HIGH -- normal range 0.45-4.5 mIU/L)
- Free T4 0.82 ng/dL (LOW -- normal range 0.89-1.76 ng/dL)

Additional:
- Anti-TPO antibodies: pending (ordered today)
- Morning cortisol: ordered (to rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency given pembro exposure)

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging today. Most recent breast MRI (post-lumpectomy surveillance at ~12 months post-surgery) showed no evidence of local or regional recurrence, post-surgical and post-radiation changes in the left upper outer quadrant, no suspicious enhancement. Annual surveillance breast MRI/mammogram will be scheduled per protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER, STAGE IIB (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II), status post neoadjuvant pembro + ddAC + weekly Taxol, left lumpectomy/ALND, adjuvant WBRT + RNI, and now completion of adjuvant Xeloda x 6 cycles:
   - She has completed all planned Xeloda. This is a significant milestone.
   - Clinical examination without evidence of disease (NED) today -- no palpable mass, no adenopathy.
   - Most recent surveillance MRI was negative.
   - Assessment: No evidence of disease at this visit.
   - Plan: Continue Keytruda adjuvant maintenance per plan (cycle 14 of planned ~17 total cycles, approximately 9 additional cycles in the adjuvant setting following neoadjuvant). Will reassess duration at cycle 17.
   - Schedule annual bilateral breast MRI + diagnostic mammogram (contralateral right breast) for surveillance.
   - Semi-annual clinical exam visits.
   - No routine tumor markers (CA 27-29 not recommended per ASCO guidelines for surveillance).

2. SUSPECTED pembro-ASSOCIATED PRIMARY HYPOTHYROIDISM (grade 1-2 irAE):
   - TSH 6.8 mIU/L (elevated), free T4 0.82 ng/dL (below normal). This is a biochemically hypothyroid pattern.
   - Clinical symptoms are consistent: fatigue (though multifactorial in this setting), weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation.
   - Thyroid exam notable for mild smooth enlargement without nodularity, no pain -- consistent with immune-mediated thyroiditis (painless/silent thyroiditis pattern, common with checkpoint inhibitors).
   - Anti-TPO antibodies sent today; morning cortisol ordered to screen for concurrent hypophysitis/adrenal insufficiency (important to rule out before treating with levothyroxine).
   - pembro does NOT need to be held for grade 1-2 hypothyroidism per NCCN/ASCO irAE guidelines.
   - Initiating levothyroxine (Synthroid) 50 mcg PO daily. Counseled patient to take on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Will recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks.
   - Endocrinology referral placed for co-management given young age and ongoing pembro exposure.
   - Patient counseled that this is a known irAE from Keytruda, generally manageable and often requiring lifelong thyroid replacement. She understood and agreed to start levothyroxine.

3. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (grade 1, bilateral feet):
   - Stable, not worsening since PTX dose reduction and completion.
   - Continue duloxetine 60 mg daily. Will continue to monitor.
   - Discussed that neuropathy may partially recover over months to years post-treatment. Some residual may be permanent.

4. GERMLINE BRCA1 PATHOGENIC VARIANT (p.E1250*):
   - Patient was re-counseled regarding ongoing implications:
     a. Contralateral breast cancer risk: approximately 40-60% lifetime risk; bilateral breast MRI + mammogram annually.
     b. Ovarian/fallopian tube cancer risk: 40-50% lifetime risk; risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) recommended ideally between ages 35-40 (or after childbearing is complete).
   - She continues to defer RRSO -- will revisit this at next visit. She was counseled that deferral beyond age 40 substantially increases ovarian cancer risk. She expressed understanding and will discuss with her partner.
   - Her sister tested positive for BRCA1 and has been referred for risk-reduction counseling per genetics.

5. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (grade 1, resolving):
   - Continue topical urea 20% cream to palms/soles as needed. Can taper pyridoxine at next visit if further improvement noted.

6. HEALTH MAINTENANCE AND SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - Dental evaluation completed (current, for zoledronic acid planning if needed in future).
   - Discussed importance of bone health given chemo-related bone loss risk: current regimen includes calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Baseline DEXA scan to be considered at next visit.
   - Tobacco: non-smoker. Alcohol: currently abstaining.
   - Psychological support: patient reports mild surveillance anxiety, coping well. Social work/counseling referral offered, deferred by patient at this time.
   - Discussed return precautions: new bone pain, unexplained weight loss, neurologic changes, shortness of breath -- present to clinic or ED promptly.

Return to clinic in approximately 6-8 weeks for pembro cycle 15/16 and thyroid recheck. Annual surveillance imaging will be coordinated at that time.

Total encounter time: 45 minutes (>50% counseling and care coordination).

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
